content.text: Cosine similarity measures the angle between two vectors, ignoring their magnitudes. Two texts encoded into vectors will have high cosine similarity if they point in the same direction in embedding space — which happens when they carry the same semantic content — regardless of how long or short the originals were.

attrs.alt: Cosine similarity geometry: two vectors a and b with angle θ, range table, and implementation code showing the dot-product shortcut when normalised.

content.text: The result is the cosine of the angle θ between the two vectors. It ranges from −1 to 1 in theory, but for most embedding models it falls in the range [0, 1] in practice because the models are trained with contrastive objectives that push unrelated texts toward orthogonal directions (cos θ ≈ 0), not opposite ones.

import numpy as np

a = np.array([0.8, 0.6, 0.0])
b = np.array([0.6, 0.8, 0.1])

# Full formula
sim = np.dot(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b))
print(f"cosine similarity: {sim:.4f}")   # 0.9606

# Pairwise similarity matrix for a batch of vectors
vecs = np.vstack([a, b, np.array([0.1, 0.0, 0.99])])

def cosine_matrix(vecs: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    norms = np.linalg.norm(vecs, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    normalised = vecs / norms
    return normalised @ normalised.T   # (N, N) matrix

print(cosine_matrix(vecs).round(3))
# [[1.    0.961 0.003]
#  [0.961 1.    0.081]
#  [0.003 0.081 1.   ]]
When vectors are pre-normalised to unit length (‖v‖ = 1), the denominator in the cosine formula becomes 1·1 = 1, and cosine similarity reduces to the plain dot product:
is used throughout the embedding examples — it unlocks dot-product search (faster) while preserving cosine semantics. FAISS
(inner product) becomes equivalent to cosine search when vectors are normalised at index time.

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import numpy as np

model = SentenceTransformer("BAAI/bge-m3")

# Normalise once at index time
vecs = model.encode(["Paris is the capital of France.",
                     "The French capital is Paris.",
                     "Python is a programming language."],
                    normalize_embeddings=True)  # ← unit norm enforced here

# Fast dot-product pairwise sim at query time
sim = vecs @ vecs.T
print(sim.round(3))
# [[1.    0.96  0.21]
#  [0.96  1.    0.22]
#  [0.21  0.22  1.  ]]

Euclidean distance (L2) measures how far apart two points are in absolute terms. Cosine similarity measures the angle between them, ignoring how far from the origin they sit. For text, magnitude is not meaningful — a long document and a short document on the same topic should score similarly to a query, even though the long one has a larger norm.

import numpy as np

# Two vectors pointing in the same direction but different magnitudes
a = np.array([3.0, 4.0])   # norm = 5
b = np.array([0.6, 0.8])   # norm = 1 (a/5)

cosine = np.dot(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b))
l2_dist = np.linalg.norm(a - b)

print(f"cosine: {cosine:.3f}")    # 1.000 — identical direction
print(f"L2 dist: {l2_dist:.3f}")  # 4.000 — far apart in absolute terms
Cosine correctly identifies these as identical in direction (same topic). L2 distance would rank them as very different, which is wrong for semantic similarity.
The thresholds that matter depend on the embedding model, but the following guidance applies broadly for modern models trained for semantic search:
These thresholds are model-dependent. Calibrate them by sampling 50–100 query-document pairs and labelling relevance by hand. Never use raw scores from one model to set thresholds for another.

from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity
import numpy as np

# Query batch vs document batch — no need to normalise first
query_vecs = np.random.randn(5, 1024)    # 5 queries
doc_vecs = np.random.randn(100, 1024)   # 100 documents

sim_matrix = cosine_similarity(query_vecs, doc_vecs)  # shape (5, 100)
print(sim_matrix.shape)   # (5, 100)

# Top-3 document indices for each query
top3 = np.argsort(sim_matrix, axis=1)[:, -3:][:, ::-1]
print(top3)  # shape (5, 3)

For large-scale pairwise computation (N > 10,000), prefer FAISS over sklearn — FAISS uses optimised BLAS routines and can distribute computation across GPU cores, reducing what would be minutes to milliseconds.

attrs.alt: Cosine similarity geometry showing direction independence and semantic search pipeline

content.text: Cosine similarity measures the cosine of the angle between two vectors in a shared embedding space. Unlike Euclidean distance, it is entirely insensitive to vector magnitude — two vectors pointing in exactly the same direction have cosine similarity 1.0 regardless of whether one is 10 times longer than the other. This property makes it the dominant similarity metric for text and multimodal embeddings, where the direction encodes meaning and the magnitude encodes frequency or confidence.

The metric was popularized in information retrieval through the TF-IDF vector space model, where documents are represented as term-frequency vectors and cosine similarity captures topical overlap independent of document length. Modern dense embedding models (BERT, Sentence-BERT, OpenAI ada-002, Cohere Embed) are explicitly trained with contrastive losses that bring semantically similar pairs to low angular distance — making cosine similarity the natural evaluation metric.

In the range [-1, 1]: a score of 1.0 means identical directions (semantically equivalent), 0 means orthogonal (unrelated), and -1 means opposite directions (conceptually opposite, common in bipolar sentiment spaces).

The central problem in semantic search and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is: given a query embedding, find the stored documents whose embeddings are most similar. Euclidean distance is magnitude-sensitive, penalizing long documents and rewarding short ones regardless of relevance. Cosine similarity removes this bias by measuring angular proximity only.

For large language model applications specifically, cosine similarity is the metric of choice for: (1) semantic search over knowledge bases in RAG pipelines, (2) finding similar examples for in-context learning, (3) deduplication of training data, (4) document clustering, and (5) embedding-based recommendation. Every major vector database — Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, Chroma — supports cosine similarity natively.
OpenAI's text-embedding-ada-002 documentation explicitly recommends cosine similarity over Euclidean distance
Sentence-BERT was trained with a triplet loss using cosine distance as the distance function
FAISS IndexFlatIP (inner product) on unit-normalised vectors is mathematically equivalent to cosine similarity and enables GPU-accelerated search
Recommendation systems at Spotify, Pinterest, and Airbnb use cosine similarity for item-item and user-item matching
Identical direction = score 1.0 regardless of vector scale. Essential when embedding magnitudes encode confidence rather than meaning.
Interpretable scores without calibration. A threshold of 0.8 means the same thing across different embedding models.
On unit-normalised vectors, cosine similarity reduces to a dot product: cos(a,b) = a·b when ‖a‖=‖b‖=1. Dot product is a single matrix multiplication.
Most text and vision-language models are trained to optimise cosine distance directly, making it the 'natural' metric.
Less affected by the curse of dimensionality than Euclidean distance for directional embeddings.

Cosine similarity's magnitude-invariance is a feature for text but a bug for some other applications. In collaborative filtering, the magnitude of a user preference vector can encode how active a user is — cosine similarity would equate a casual user who rated one movie 5-stars with a power user who rated 1000 movies 5-stars. Dot product (without normalisation) is often better in this setting because magnitude acts as a popularity prior.

Cosine similarity also has no notion of absolute distance in the embedding space — two pairs of vectors can both have cosine similarity 0.9 but be geometrically very different. For anomaly detection or threshold-based filtering, this can make score calibration across datasets difficult.
Bad for recommendation when magnitude = engagement
0.8 may mean very different things for different encoders
Calibrate thresholds empirically on validation set
Not a metric in the strict sense
Use cosine distance (1 − cos) for clustering requiring a true metric
Can't model asymmetric similarity (question vs. answer)
Extra compute at ingest and query time
Normalise once at embedding time; negligible overhead
How Cosine Similarity Works: Deep Mechanics
Starting from the dot product identity:
Dividing both sides by the product of norms isolates cos θ:

attrs.latex: \text{cosine\_similarity}(a, b) = \frac{a \cdot b}{\|a\|_2\,\|b\|_2} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^d a_i b_i}{\sqrt{\sum_i a_i^2}\,\sqrt{\sum_i b_i^2}}

content.text: The result is the cosine of the angle between the two vectors, which depends only on their directions. Normalising both vectors to unit length first converts this to a pure dot product — the most common implementation:
Cosine similarity is not a proper distance metric because it doesn't satisfy the triangle inequality. Cosine distance (also called angular distance) converts it to a valid metric:
The angular distance (in radians) is a stricter metric used in some clustering applications:
When to Use Cosine Similarity — and When Not To
Text embedding search: semantic search, RAG retrieval, document deduplication
Any embedding model whose documentation specifies cosine similarity
High-dimensional sparse vectors (TF-IDF) where magnitude encodes term frequency, not meaning
Cross-lingual retrieval where multilingual encoders map semantically equivalent text to similar directions
Deduplication: pairs with cosine ≥ 0.98 are near-duplicates for most models
Prefer Dot Product or Euclidean When
Magnitude matters: recommendation systems where high-engagement users should have higher scores
Model trained with dot product loss (ANN retrieval for asymmetric use cases)
Comparing within a bounded embedding space where Euclidean distance is meaningful

import numpy as np

def cosine_similarity(a: np.ndarray, b: np.ndarray) -> float:
    """Cosine similarity between 1D vectors a and b."""
    dot   = np.dot(a, b)
    norm_a = np.linalg.norm(a)
    norm_b = np.linalg.norm(b)
    if norm_a == 0 or norm_b == 0:
        return 0.0  # undefined for zero vectors; treat as maximally dissimilar
    return dot / (norm_a * norm_b)

a = np.array([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
b = np.array([2.0, 4.0, 6.0])  # same direction, double magnitude
c = np.array([1.0, 0.0, 0.0])  # orthogonal to components of a

print(f"cos(a, b) = {cosine_similarity(a, b):.6f}")  # 1.000000 — same direction
print(f"cos(a, c) = {cosine_similarity(a, c):.6f}")  # 0.267261 — partially similar
print(f"cos(a,-a) = {cosine_similarity(a, -a):.6f}") # -1.000000 — opposite

import numpy as np

def batch_cosine_similarity(A: np.ndarray, B: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    """
    Compute cosine similarity between all pairs of rows in A (n x d) and B (m x d).
    Returns (n x m) similarity matrix. Normalises first for efficiency.
    """
    # Normalise each row to unit length
    A_norm = A / np.linalg.norm(A, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    B_norm = B / np.linalg.norm(B, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    # Dot product of normalised vectors = cosine similarity
    return A_norm @ B_norm.T   # (n, m)

# 3 queries vs 5 documents, 128-dimensional embeddings
queries   = np.random.randn(3, 128).astype(np.float32)
documents = np.random.randn(5, 128).astype(np.float32)

sim_matrix = batch_cosine_similarity(queries, documents)  # (3, 5)
print("Similarity matrix shape:", sim_matrix.shape)
print("Best document per query:", np.argmax(sim_matrix, axis=1))
Step 3 — Using sentence-transformers for Semantic Similarity

# pip install sentence-transformers
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, util
import numpy as np

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")  # 384-d, fast, good quality

sentences = [
    "Machine learning is a subset of artificial intelligence.",
    "AI includes algorithms that learn from data.",
    "The Eiffel Tower is located in Paris.",
    "Deep learning uses neural networks with many layers.",
    "France is known for its rich cultural heritage.",
]

embeddings = model.encode(sentences, normalize_embeddings=True)  # already unit-normed

# Pairwise cosine similarities (= dot products since normalised)
sim_matrix = util.cos_sim(embeddings, embeddings)
print("Similarity matrix:")
print(np.round(sim_matrix.numpy(), 2))

# Top-2 most similar pairs
pairs = []
for i in range(len(sentences)):
    for j in range(i+1, len(sentences)):
        pairs.append((float(sim_matrix[i][j]), i, j))

for score, i, j in sorted(pairs, reverse=True)[:3]:
    print(f"  {score:.3f}: '{sentences[i][:40]}' ≈ '{sentences[j][:40]}'")

Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) uses cosine similarity to find the most relevant context passages before passing them to an LLM. The pipeline embeds a knowledge base offline, then at query time embeds the question and retrieves the top-k most similar passages.

import numpy as np
from typing import List, Tuple

# Simulated production RAG retrieval
class CosineRetriever:
    def __init__(self, embed_dim: int = 384):
        self.embed_dim = embed_dim
        self.embeddings: np.ndarray = np.empty((0, embed_dim), dtype=np.float32)
        self.texts: List[str] = []

    def add_documents(self, texts: List[str], embeddings: np.ndarray) -> None:
        """Add pre-computed, L2-normalised embeddings to the index."""
        assert embeddings.shape[1] == self.embed_dim
        # Normalise to unit sphere — cosine = dot product from here on
        norms = np.linalg.norm(embeddings, axis=1, keepdims=True)
        normed = embeddings / np.maximum(norms, 1e-8)
        self.embeddings = np.vstack([self.embeddings, normed]) if len(self.embeddings) else normed
        self.texts.extend(texts)

    def retrieve(self, query_emb: np.ndarray, k: int = 5) -> List[Tuple[float, str]]:
        """Return top-k documents by cosine similarity."""
        # Normalise query
        q = query_emb / max(np.linalg.norm(query_emb), 1e-8)
        # Dot product = cosine similarity (both unit-normed)
        scores = self.embeddings @ q                       # (N,)
        top_k_idx = np.argpartition(scores, -k)[-k:]      # fast top-k
        top_k_idx = top_k_idx[np.argsort(scores[top_k_idx])[::-1]]
        return [(float(scores[i]), self.texts[i]) for i in top_k_idx]


# Demo
np.random.seed(0)
retriever = CosineRetriever(embed_dim=128)

# Offline: embed and index documents
docs = [f"Document about topic {i}" for i in range(1000)]
doc_embs = np.random.randn(1000, 128).astype(np.float32)
retriever.add_documents(docs, doc_embs)

# Online: query
query_embedding = np.random.randn(128).astype(np.float32)
results = retriever.retrieve(query_embedding, k=5)
print("Top-5 retrieved documents:")
for score, text in results:
    print(f"  cosine={score:.4f}  {text}")

In production, this pattern is wrapped by a vector database (Chroma, Pinecone, Qdrant) that handles persistence, indexing, and approximate search. The cosine similarity computation is unchanged — the database just makes it faster at scale via HNSW or IVF indexing.
Using cosine on unnormalised vectors when dot product is intended
Magnitude discarded — high-engagement items ranked same as low-engagement
Use unnormalised dot product for magnitude-sensitive ranking
Applying cosine to models trained with L2 loss
Wrong metric for the embedding space; poor retrieval quality
Check model card — use the metric the model was trained with
Division by zero → NaN scores that propagate silently
Add a small epsilon: norm = max(norm, 1e-8); or filter zero vectors at ingest
Thresholding cosine scores without per-model calibration
Score of 0.8 means different things for different models
Evaluate recall at different thresholds on your task before deploying
Using sklearn cosine_similarity on millions of pairs naively
Full NxM matrix in RAM — OOM for large N, M
Use chunked batch processing or a vector database

import numpy as np

# WRONG: zero vector causes NaN
a = np.array([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
zero = np.zeros(3)

# This produces NaN silently in many implementations
sim_wrong = np.dot(a, zero) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(zero))
print(f"Wrong: {sim_wrong}")   # nan

# CORRECT: guard against zero norm
def safe_cosine(a: np.ndarray, b: np.ndarray) -> float:
    norm_a = np.linalg.norm(a)
    norm_b = np.linalg.norm(b)
    if norm_a < 1e-8 or norm_b < 1e-8:
        return 0.0  # undefined; return maximally dissimilar
    return float(np.dot(a, b) / (norm_a * norm_b))

print(f"Safe:  {safe_cosine(a, zero)}")  # 0.0
Normalise embeddings at ingest time, not at query time:
Pre-normalise the database once; then every query needs only a dot product. This cuts query latency significantly at scale.
Verify the model's intended metric before deployment:
Most HuggingFace model cards list the similarity function under 'Usage'. Default to cosine for text; check image models case by case.
Use sentence-transformers util.cos_sim for batch operations:
It handles normalisation, batching, and returns a tensor suitable for direct use with PyTorch or numpy.
Run precision/recall evaluation at cosine thresholds 0.7, 0.75, 0.8, 0.85, 0.9, 0.95 on a labelled validation set before picking a cutoff.
Values above 0.98 reliably indicate near-duplicate or paraphrased content for most modern sentence embedding models.
If average cosine similarity drifts, it signals distribution shift in the query population or a change in the embedding model.

On unit-normalised vectors, cosine similarity reduces to a dot product — a BLAS Level 3 operation that runs near peak hardware speed on both CPU and GPU. For a single query against N database vectors of dimension d, the cost is O(N·d) floating point multiply-adds — identical to Euclidean and dot product search.

GPU acceleration via FAISS IndexFlatIP (inner product = cosine on unit-normed vectors) achieves ~1B comparisons per second per GPU card. At 10M vectors of dimension 768, a single A100 GPU returns exact top-10 results in under 5ms. For larger indexes, approximate HNSW or IVF reduces this to O(log N) at the cost of 1–5% recall.

import numpy as np
import faiss

d, N = 768, 1_000_000
# Normalise embeddings — now IndexFlatIP computes cosine similarity
db = np.random.randn(N, d).astype("float32")
faiss.normalize_L2(db)  # in-place L2 normalisation

index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(d)  # inner product = cosine for unit-norm vectors
index.add(db)

query = np.random.randn(10, d).astype("float32")
faiss.normalize_L2(query)
scores, indices = index.search(query, k=10)
# scores[i] = cosine similarities (in [-1, 1]) for query i
print("Top cosine scores (query 0):", scores[0])
Exact cosine search on unit-normed vectors. Call faiss.normalize_L2(arr) before indexing. Supports GPU.
Batch cosine similarity tensor returning torch.Tensor. Handles normalisation internally.
Dense matrix output for small datasets; not suitable for N > 100k without chunking.
Vector databases with native cosine index type; handle normalisation, persistence, and HNSW indexing automatically.
Embedded vector database default to cosine similarity; ideal for local RAG prototyping.
Tree-based ANN with angular distance (= cosine distance) as a native metric. pip install annoy.

# Chroma — simplest cosine RAG store
# pip install chromadb
import chromadb

client = chromadb.Client()
col = client.create_collection("docs", metadata={"hnsw:space": "cosine"})

col.add(
    documents=["Neural networks learn from data", "Transformers use attention"],
    ids=["doc1", "doc2"],
)

results = col.query(
    query_texts=["deep learning models"],
    n_results=2,
)
print(results["documents"])  # ranked by cosine similarity
Models trained with L2 loss, clustering, CLIP images
Set overlap, binary features, near-duplicate detection
Binary codes, LSH hashing, error correction

The dominant practical choice is: use cosine similarity for all text and multimodal embedding models; use dot product for recommendation/ranking models where magnitude encodes preference strength; use Euclidean distance only when the model was explicitly trained with L2-based loss.

Cosine similarity is used to detect jailbreak attempts and prompt injection in LLM safety systems: a safety classifier embeds the user query and computes cosine similarity against a library of known harmful queries. High similarity triggers a safety check. Adversaries respond by crafting inputs that are semantically equivalent but directionally different in embedding space — a phenomenon that motivates adversarial robustness research in embedding models.

In production retrieval systems, cosine similarity scores can be manipulated by embedding injection: adversarial documents engineered to have high cosine similarity with common queries regardless of their actual content. This is mitigated by combining semantic similarity with lexical reranking (BM25 hybrid search) and content filtering.
Validate score ranges at ingest and query time:
Assert scores lie in [-1, 1]; NaN or out-of-range values indicate unnormalised vectors or zero-norm inputs.
Use hybrid search for security-sensitive retrieval:
Combine cosine semantic similarity with keyword (BM25) reranking to reduce susceptibility to adversarial embeddings.
If retrieved chunks suddenly have cosine ≥ 0.99 for all queries, investigate for data poisoning or index corruption.
Repeated query patterns with gradually decreasing cosine thresholds may indicate an adversary probing the embedding space.
Cosine similarity measures the angle between vectors; it is completely insensitive to magnitude — a critical property for text embeddings where direction encodes meaning.
The formula is cos(θ) = (a·b) / (‖a‖·‖b‖). On unit-normalised vectors it reduces to a dot product — the most efficient possible computation.
Scores range from -1 (opposite directions) to 1 (identical directions); 0 means orthogonal / unrelated.
Use cosine when the model card says cosine similarity (true for most text embedding models: Sentence-BERT, OpenAI ada-002, Cohere Embed).
Pre-normalise database vectors at ingest time so every query becomes a fast dot product; use FAISS IndexFlatIP for this pattern.
Guard against zero-norm vectors at both ingest and query time — division by zero produces silent NaN propagation.
Calibrate similarity thresholds empirically for your task and model; do not transfer thresholds from a different encoder.
For large-scale retrieval (> 1M vectors), use approximate methods (HNSW, IVF) with FAISS IndexFlatIP or a managed vector database.
